author: @ogi22
text:
Naming things in a different way, so we can avoid taxes/regulations/formal problems.
We had the same thing in one company while building a conveyor system. We had to put a lift in the system, but customer didn't want it to be supervised by the Office of Technical Inspection. So we added a short conveyer part, so the lift was "loaded and unloaded automatically" (no direct human-machine interface), and we called it "a vertical part of a horizontal transfer system". Works till this day 😁

author: @daveh6356
text:
A recent visit to the UK taught me the driving public there needs to come to a realisation as they have serious range/charge anxiety. It stems from not being used to home-charging and thinking they need a weekly charge at an external 'refuelling' point rather than a nightly charge at home. This leads to them thinking they need huge batteries with hundreds of miles of range (car marketing panders to/exacerbates this) rather than looking at their actually daily range and realising current & even smaller batteries will be fine. We've seen this in the large EVs being rolled out first, once the 'hot hatch' EVs come, smaller batteries & lower daily range will be accepted.

Most already made this leap once from feature phones lasting a week to smartphones only lasting 1-2 days bringing on charge anxiety, it'll be the same with cars. PHEVs are a neither/neither and will damage any shift to EVs, my prediction - they'll last one generation only, until the realisation hits.

author: @johnbarry8185
text:
Hey Rob, I saw something about this engine several weeks or months ago, and the one thing that kept coming to my mind was, the slope of the 'cam' should have been much more steep.  It appears that the pistons will be pushing against a cam that is very shallow.  
On another note, I wanted to let you know(you may already) about what I consider to be THE BEST motor to turn into a generator.  That is the turntable motor from a microwave.  At 120v(I'm in the US), these motors run VERY SLOWLY.  If a motor runs at 6 RPMs, with 120 volts, we should be able to turn the motor 20, 30, or more RPMs, and get a decent voltage.  In fact, that is EXACTLY what happens.  These motors are THE BEST generators, with NO modifications.  I have one, that I mounted in the middle of a 5- gallon bucket lid.  Just gently spinning the lid will produce VERY GOOD voltages.  Give it a try, and let me know.
Keep up the good work, mate.

author: @donaldhobson8873
text:
I think the "there aren't enough resources in the world" figures are generally nonsense. Or at least I haven't seen a good one. 
Usually these figures are based on reserves. Some parts of the world, chemists have gone and measured lots of lithium. These are the reserves. But much of the world, no one has measured one way or the other. The world is big and there aren't that many mining chemists. It's highly unlikely that the rest of the world is totally devoid of lithium, but it gets counted as 0, because the chemists haven't been there yet.

Also, proven reserves tends to only reference what can be extracted economically. If the price of lithium goes up, or better mining tech is developed, new mines full of low quality ore become worth mining.

author: @pandemik0
text:
The internal combustion engine will live on in motorsport, classic cars etc. The horse wasn't made extinct by the motorcar. Love this channel but the idea there is any kind of material limitations to the renewable energy revolution is the fossil fuel industry's fantasy they wish were true. It's a hard road getting there but it's all viable. For example, an EV requires about 10-15kg of lithium, assuming not technical advances, well, an ICE engine car needs 10-15kg of lead in it's battery. So the entire global litihum supply chain merely needs to scale to the level of what we already did with lead. Huh, is that it? Both have similar abundance in earth's crust too. We never had a problem with lead or many other materials. We've also never ever had an problem with scaling anything in the history of industrial revolution. Copper could be a problem, but it turns out aluminum can subistitute in many cases, and it seems like other exotic conductors might be on the horizon. Rare earth materials will fall out of use with iron-nickel based magnets.

If there's a problem with lithium we can use aluminium, magnesium and sodium as alternatives which are too cheap and abundant to bother doing calculations.This is why the fossil fuel industry is increasing panicky.

text: The biggest problem that I can see with a conventional internal combustion powered car is that most the time the engine is to large for the the job, what I mean by that is when used around town you might be only using 30KW or less with an engine that can produce 110KW which is an inefficient way to use an engine. author: @Spoods117
text:
I've spent a great deal of time looking into fuel efficient vehicles and have discovered the Nissan e-power, which appears to be a series hybrid, small engine up front connected to a generator which charges a 2ish kilowatt hour battery pack that feeds the electric motor and wheels all the angry pixies, this design in my opinion (whatever that's worth haha!) is superior to parallel hybrids because of the single most important factor, decoupling the powerplant from the driven wheels, an engines efficiency is not static, its wildly dynamic, but thats not to say we cant identify more efficient points in the RPM band which is what the series hybrid does best, the engine (powerplant) is free to cycle up or down its power production irrespective of what the driven wheels are doing where if they were coupled to the powerplant it then becomes a slave to gearing and wheel speed and so is the efficiency of converting the potential chemical energy into kinetic energy is heavily reduced, series can meet or exceed the demand to drive and charge, giving far greater range of fuel conversion strategies.
I have thought about attempting a diy model using a lawn mower engine, testing the fuel efficiency would be, target a range of specific rpms, hold at each, measure power generated and fuel flow and plot them on a graph, once determined an electric throttle body can be used to control the rpm and some sort of arduino would be used to control the operation of the throttle body.

If we plug in renewables over time and demand for powerplants decreases, entire recycling and construction/service industry would open up, bringing opportunities, but the people aren't allowed to be anything but customers.. so it's up to ourselves to learn to build our own infrastructure for ourselves and each other, move out of the cities and build our own existence to take back control of our own destinies.

How about a VAWT turned horizontal on a car roof as a "range extender"?
Even if it only harvest a fraction of the air blowing over a car, it does that while the car stands or drives. How much m/s do you have at average driving speeds?
10 inch height over the breadth of a car... should recuperate quite some energy while driving and harvest some while parking. Especially if you don't use permanent magnets but coils on both rotor and stator. Which could also regulate the max speed it rotates at

replies.author: @danedmiston9673
replies.text: Any wind turbine added to a vehicle would add drag.  Of the the extra energy needed to overcome that drag,  about 40% is lost on the wind that just gets out of the way of the turbine.  Only 59.3% (Betz's law) of the extra energy expended by the vehicle is available to the turbine.  Even more of that energy would be lost from turbine and power transmission inefficiencies.

replies.author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
replies.text:
@danedmiston9673 
Ik, Ik, and Thermodynamics tell us that 110% aren't possible, but thats beside the point. Its about that even if only a part, a fraction is recovered while driving, the extended range comes from the fact that most cars are parked most of the time and that a turbine then could still gather a fair bit of that "free energy"

author: @hissst69
text:
There has never been a problem with the Internal Combustion Engine. The problem is Rockefeller making everyone use Petrol. Just use alcohol for a totally clean burn (all the hydrogen, none of the carbons). Take off the alcohol taxes he introduced in most countries and walla, cheap, renewable energy.. made up problem is gone.. there is no global warming anyway (check out how much pollution one volcano erupting does) but of course temperatures are cyclical so they can claim whatever they want. There is no pandemic of pollution and if there was alcohol is the answer and has been for well over 100 years.

In the states, in 1899 you could produce a gallon of alcohol for 10 cents, the federal tax was over 2 dollars per gallon. This is how he killed his competition, he also owned newspapers that promoted booze induced accidents and family problems, this lead to the prohibition, when the last US farm producing fuel died the prohibition ended.. almost the same day.. there is no problem to solve but the one he made, all cars from the 90s and up can already run on booze, E85 fuel is 85% alcohol, and it says right on the pump not to use cars older than 96' I believe (rubber hoses and gaskets melted). Although Ford was making its motor to run on anything until the 40's.. he also killed the electric car in around 1915's to 1920's... Right when they were getting about the same range as a Tesla today. but, I digress.


Anyway, it's fun to make stuff.. as long as people don't get serious about it.. I do love me some inventioning :)

replies.author: @donaldhobson8873
replies.text:
The problem with that is the grid to wheel efficiency will inevitably suck. 
An electric car could well get a grid to wheel efficiency of 75% or more. 
Green hydrogen in an IC engine will be lucky to get 20%.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @donaldhobson8873It depends on how much sabotage is involved, for example you can obtain hydrogen from biomass (including waste) easily and use it as fuel directly (maybe with some losses due to storage), but if you try to generate electricity from the biomass then transmit it and recharge an EV, there is a huge loss involved. Something similar happens with fossil fuels. Each energy source is different but EVs are very efficient only when you use  sources such as wind or hydro power because the conversion process is easier. Even then, EVs are heavier and this also reduces their overall efficiency.

replies.author: @donaldhobson8873
replies.text:
@vitordelima Firstly, the amount of spare biomass lying around isn't enough to run the worlds cars easily. 
And given a source of biomass, it is more efficient to burn it in a power station and charge an EV than it is to make hydrogen and run a hydrogen ICE. Firstly, making hydrogen wastes a lot of your energy. Those bioreactor bacteria need energy to run.  And produce substances other than hydrogen. 
Also, power plants can be quite a lot more efficient than ICE, at the cost of being bigger and heavier. 
EV's can be slightly heavier, or not. Batteries are heavier than fuel tanks, but motors are ligher, and gear boxes can be removed altogether on some EV designs. But a small difference in weight makes a small difference in efficiency.  
EV's + Photovoltaic solar are the most efficient way to use sunshine to move you places.

author: @saiello2061
text: Given evidence that battery development appears to be fast approaching an asymptotic limit, it would seem that pure EV's will never be able to compete with ICE vehicles. That's to say nothing about the necessary charging infrastructure that will be needed but has no current solution in the offing. In my opinion, hybrids will be the compromise solution that will allow them to compete. But you don't have to use a conventional or even unconventional piston engine in a hybrid. Attempts have been made to make jet engined cars but they have failed because of the expensive transmissions needed to reduce the high speeds of the jet engine to manageable road speeds. Enter the hybrid Electric Jet car. Use conventional EV battery technology and a very small jet engine to charge the battery as a range extender. This immediately solves the jet to road coupling problem as the jet's power is decoupled from the road via the battery. Turbine engines can be more efficient than piston engines when they are run at fixed optimum speeds and you will only be running the turbine at fixed speeds to charge a battery. A turbine generator will also beat an Inn engine generator in terms of footprint. At least one of the guys ( maybe both ) that started Tesla ( not Musk! ) are on another venture to develop just such a vehicle. All solutions are compromises, I think the hydrid electric is just the solution needed here for our immediate electric future.... 👍

author: @mikaelfransson3658
text: Are that sheating to put an Water-based HHO in a ICE driven car? That we can fix tomorrow! The problem still is remaining! Like pollution from metal's! Yes the double one stroke is a option but it take app. same amount fuel as ordinary ICE, but it weight less and are smaller! I love your search for solutions Rob! So big thanks!! /Mikael

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text:
I think you may have used google-translate? Or predictive text is messing youi about as usual. 
HHO shows promise, but seperating hydrogen from water is currently expensive and inneficient, and the engines that run on it have terrible performance. 
That's before you get to hugely increased maintainance costs (Hydrogen eats metal) 
And of course what do you need if you were making HHO onboard? A BATTERY, that will need to be recharged more often and replaced more often. 
I am not saying it's impossible, but "tomorrow" is simply not true, it is very much a developing technology.

replies.author: @mikaelfransson3658
replies.text: @TheBaconWizard Your right Sheating might be be better English! And you also right about HHO or brown-gas. But in the motor is near the same problem as ordinary gas! If I say it can be cheap enough to drive a car on HHO! The secret in the process is iron-ions in the water. Just plain rust and after put it in a microwave similar-process! But the motor Robert talks about is called a one Stroke engine but isn't. It's a two stroke engine! and for me is this okay if we can find right fuel, like methane.  /Mikael
